1990.assessment: Civil war; international community engaged with ECOWAS/ECOMOG intervention but Liberia increasingly isolated. Doe government had limited international legitimacy.

1991.assessment: IGNU nominally recognized; NPFL controls territory. Liberia in limbo diplomatically.

1992.assessment: UN SC discussions; ECOWAS managing. Not pariah but significant isolation.

1993.assessment: Cotonou process with UN and ECOWAS engagement but significant isolation.

1994.assessment: Transitional governments; limited diplomatic engagement.

1995.assessment: Abuja process; some diplomatic activity.

1996.assessment: Abuja II; some improvement but still limited.

1997.assessment: Taylor elected; internationally recognized despite reservations. Carter Center monitored elections. Some normalization of relations. US still cautious about Taylor.

1998.assessment: Taylor government internationally engaged; some diplomatic acceptance but growing concern about Sierra Leone support.

1999.assessment: Taylor accused of supporting RUF in Sierra Leone; international pressure mounting. LURD insurgency backed by Guinea.

2000.assessment: Growing international isolation over Sierra Leone. UN Panel of Experts investigation. US and EU increasingly critical.

2001.assessment: UN sanctions (UNSCR 1343); Taylor indicted for war crimes before Special Court for Sierra Leone. Significant international isolation.

2002.assessment: More UN sanctions; ICC/SCSL pressure. Taylor increasingly isolated.

2003.assessment: Taylor formally indicted by SCSL (March 2003) while in Ghana for peace talks. LURD/MODEL siege. Taylor exiled to Nigeria August 2003. CPA signed; NTGL installed. International pariah replaced by transitional legitimacy.

2004.assessment: NTGL internationally supported; UNMIL; donor conferences. Sanctions continue but Liberia receiving reconstruction support. US, EU, UN engaged.

2005.assessment: EJS wins elections; major international enthusiasm. US visits (Bush administration). World Bank/IMF actively engaged. Broad international support.

2006.assessment: EJS inaugurated; G8 debt relief; IMF PRGF; World Bank IDA. Major donor conferences in Washington. Liberia becomes model for post-conflict reconstruction.

2007.assessment: Continued full international engagement; ECOWAS, AU, UN, bilateral donors. US Embassy fully staffed; Liberia has broad diplomatic presence.

2008.assessment: Full diplomatic engagement. HIPC process. US Congressional interest.

2009.assessment: Full international integration. EJS as international spokesperson for West Africa governance.

2010.assessment: HIPC completion; debt relief. Full international engagement.

2011.assessment: EJS Nobel Peace Prize (Oct 2011). Liberia highly visible internationally. Full engagement.

2012.assessment: Continued full engagement. Taylor convicted at SCSL (March 2012).

2013.assessment: Full integration; EITI compliant; good governance reputation.

2014.assessment: Ebola brings massive international response; WHO, MSF, US military, UK military. Full engagement.

2015.assessment: Post-Ebola; continued engagement. EJS tours internationally.

2016.assessment: All sanctions lifted; fully integrated. UNMIL drawdown.

2017.assessment: Peaceful elections; international praise. Smooth transition.

2018.assessment: UNMIL exits; Weah government fully recognized. Full diplomatic relations.

2019.assessment: Economic crisis; MCA suspended; some concerns about Weah governance. Broad but slightly strained engagement.

2020.assessment: COVID-19 international support. Full engagement.

2021.assessment: Full international integration; ECOWAS active member.

2022.assessment: Full relations.

2023.assessment: Peaceful elections praised internationally. Full engagement.

2024.assessment: Boakai government; full international relations. AU, ECOWAS, UN engaged.

2025.assessment: Estimated full diplomatic integration maintained.

1990.assessment: ECOMOG (ECOWAS Monitoring Group) deploys August 1990; ~3,000 troops initially. ECOMOG effectively dominant military actor in Monrovia and coastal areas. Establishing beachhead against NPFL.

1991.assessment: ECOMOG ~7,500 troops in Liberia. Dominant in Monrovia. Nigeria leads ECOMOG with Ghana, Guinea-Bissau, Sierra Leone, Gambia. ECOMOG controls port and airport.

1992.assessment: ECOMOG active combat vs NPFL during Operation Octopus (October 1992). ECOMOG ~9,000. Active combat operations to repel NPFL Monrovia assault.

1993.assessment: ECOMOG active operations; UN Observer Mission (UNOMIL) deployed (September 1993) with ~300 military observers. Active foreign combat.

1994.assessment: ECOMOG and UNOMIL; continued active operations. Multiple ceasefire violations.

1995.assessment: ECOMOG ~12,000 troops; active operations. Abuja peace process.

1996.assessment: ECOMOG active during April 1996 Monrovia fighting. ~10,000 troops. ECOMOG helps restore some order after April catastrophe.

1997.assessment: Post-election; ECOMOG reducing presence. Taylor government. ECOMOG ~8,000; transitioning from combat to monitoring/peacekeeping.

1998.assessment: ECOMOG ~6,000; declining further. Monitoring role.

1999.assessment: LURD insurgency (backed by Guinea); Guinea providing cross-border support to LURD. ECOMOG still present but declining. Active foreign involvement.

2000.assessment: ECOMOG ~3,000 still; Guinean support to LURD. Active cross-border operations.

2001.assessment: ECOMOG withdrawing further; Guinea supporting LURD. Active foreign military involvement against Taylor.

2002.assessment: ECOMOG minimal; MODEL from Côte d'Ivoire; Guinea supporting LURD. Multiple foreign military actors active.

2003.assessment: ECOMOG mostly gone; Nigerian/US evacuation forces briefly present. ECOWAS brigade (ECOMIL) deploys July 2003. UNMIL authorized October 2003 (UNSCR 1509). US Navy offshore but not landing. Active operations.

2004.assessment: UNMIL fully deployed: ~15,000 troops + ~1,000 military observers; one of largest UN peacekeeping operations. Nigeria, India, Pakistan, Senegal, Bangladesh troops.

2005.assessment: UNMIL ~15,000; peacekeeping/stabilization. Supporting DDR and elections.

2006.assessment: UNMIL ~15,000; EJS government; peacekeeping mission critical to security.

1990.assessment: Catastrophic displacement: ~20% of population (500,000+) fled as civil war engulfed country. Refugees to Guinea, Côte d'Ivoire, Sierra Leone. IDPs ~700,000 inside Liberia. Worst humanitarian crisis in West Africa.

1991.assessment: ~25% population displaced. Refugees abroad ~600,000; IDPs ~700,000. Multiple factions committing atrocities; entire communities fleeing.

1992.assessment: Operation Octopus creates new displacement. ~20% of population displaced internationally. Monrovia fighting.

1993.assessment: Continued displacement; some return to ceasefire areas. ~15% population as refugees.

1994.assessment: Ongoing high displacement. ~12% refugees. Multiple faction areas.

1995.assessment: Still catastrophic; Abuja peace allows some returns but insecurity prevents large-scale return.

1996.assessment: April 1996 Monrovia fighting creates new displacement; ~12% population as refugees. Some returns from 1995 reversed.

1997.assessment: Post-election mass returns begin; Taylor government provides relative peace. Refugees returning from Guinea, Côte d'Ivoire. ~6% still abroad.

1998.assessment: Continued returns; UNHCR voluntary repatriation program. ~3% population still refugees.

1999.assessment: LURD insurgency begins; renewed displacement in north. Some returnees re-displaced. ~4%.

2000.assessment: Second Civil War; new waves of displacement. ~6% population displaced.

2001.assessment: Escalating conflict; ~8% population as refugees/IDPs.

2002.assessment: MODEL opens second front; ~12% population displaced.

2003.assessment: Worst year of Second Civil War; Monrovia siege. ~18% population displaced; 500,000+ IDPs. Taylor exile; UNMIL arrives.

2004.assessment: UNMIL present; returns beginning but slow. ~12% still displaced.

2005.assessment: UNHCR voluntary repatriation program; significant returns. Elections encourage returns. ~6% still displaced.

2006.assessment: EJS government; rapid returns. UNHCR repatriation operations. ~3% still abroad.

2007.assessment: Continued returns; ~1.5% still displaced. Majority have returned.

2008.assessment: Near-end of displacement; <1% remaining refugees. Some residual UNHCR caseload.

2009.assessment: Residual displacement; UNHCR closing refugee camps.

2010.assessment: Minimal displacement remaining. Some stateless persons. Returns essentially complete.

2011.assessment: Post-election violence creates brief new displacement of a few hundred. Overall stable.

2014.assessment: Ebola creates some fear-driven internal movement but not large-scale displacement.

2015.assessment: Ebola ending; stable.

2018.assessment: Stable.

2019.assessment: Stable; some Côte d'Ivoire residual returns.

2020.assessment: COVID-19; minimal displacement.

2021.assessment: Stable.

2022.assessment: Stable.

2023.assessment: Stable; peaceful elections.

2024.assessment: Stable.

2025.assessment: Estimated stable; minimal displacement.

1990.assessment: Complete fragmentation: Samuel Doe (AFL/Krahn), Charles Taylor (NPFL/Gio-Mano), Prince Johnson (INPFL, breaks from Taylor). Ethnically structured factions. Total elite fragmentation.

1991.assessment: ULIMO forms from Doe loyalists (Krahn/Mandingo) vs NPFL. IGNU rump government in Monrovia. Four+ competing elite factions.

1992.assessment: ULIMO splits into ULIMO-K (Mandingo) and ULIMO-J (Krahn); LPC forms in southeast. Six+ competing factions, each with ethnic base.

1993.assessment: Six-faction LNTG; each faction nominally represented but no shared agenda. Complete fragmentation.

1994.assessment: LNTG II with 6 factions; constant ceasefire violations. Elite completely fragmented.

1995.assessment: Abuja accords attempt to create Council of State with faction leaders; still fragmented.

1996.assessment: April 1996 street battles between Roosevelt Johnson (ULIMO-J) and Taylor (NPFL) factions in Monrovia. Fragmentation continues despite Abuja II.

1997.assessment: Taylor wins 75% of votes; NPP dominant. Opposition (Sawyer, Ellen Johnson Sirleaf) present but marginalized. Significant internal NPP tensions between armed/civilian factions.

1998.assessment: Roosevelt Johnson arrested; Taylor consolidating but facing armed challenger groups. AFL vs ex-ULIMO tensions. Active internal power struggles.

1999.assessment: LURD insurgency begins; Taylor faces armed opposition. Internal government tensions as some security officials suspected of disloyalty.

2000.assessment: Taylor increasingly paranoid; purges within AFL and security services. Active internal power struggles plus external military challenge.

2001.assessment: Taylor purges real and perceived opponents. Lofa Defense Force (anti-LURD local militia) complicates command structure.

2002.assessment: MODEL opens second front; Taylor's inner circle fragmenting. Some defections to opposition.

2003.assessment: Taylor exile; multiple factions (LURD, MODEL, NPP remnants, AFL) contesting power. CPA creates power-sharing NTGL. Complete fragmentation in transition.

2004.assessment: NTGL (Gyude Bryant); factional tensions within transitional government; ex-faction leaders competing for influence. Significant power struggles.

2005.assessment: Elections 2005: multi-party competitive; EJS (Unity Party) wins. Coalition government forming. Some tensions but transitioning to functional governance.

2006.assessment: EJS government; broad coalition including international supporters. Minor tensions within Unity Party but functional governance. Opposition active.

2007.assessment: EJS managing elite coalition; some factionalism within government. CDC (Congress for Democratic Change) growing as opposition.

2008.assessment: Stable; EJS government managing. Some tensions over GEMAP oversight perceived as external interference.

2009.assessment: Minor tensions; opposition CDC consolidating. Political system maturing.

2010.assessment: EJS managing coalition; parliamentary elections; Unity Party maintaining dominance with some opposition.

2011.assessment: EJS re-elected; CDC boycott run-off creates tension but resolved. Elite broadly functional.

2012.assessment: Stable; EJS second term. Some intraparty tensions but functional.

2013.assessment: Continued minor tensions; opposition organizing for 2017.

2014.assessment: Ebola creates national unity but also some political tensions over response.

2015.assessment: Post-Ebola; EJS lame duck term. Succession politics beginning.

2016.assessment: Electoral preparations; multiple parties organizing. Healthy competition.

2017.assessment: Competitive elections; multiple parties. First round requires run-off. George Weah (CDC) vs Joseph Boakai (Unity Party). Peaceful.

2018.assessment: Weah government; some concerns about inexperience; technocrats in key roles. CDC-led government. Minor tensions with opposition.

2019.assessment: Weah government crisis: economic difficulties, MCA (Millennium Challenge Account) terminated; massive protests; Weah government stability questioned. Active power struggles within CDC.

2020.assessment: COVID-19 creates some unity; tensions reduced. Weah government stabilizes somewhat.

2021.assessment: Boakai (ex-VP) organizing opposition; some divisions within CDC. Minor tensions functional.

2022.assessment: Pre-election; opposition (Boakai/Unity Party) vs Weah (CDC). Competitive but functional.

2023.assessment: October elections; Boakai wins; Weah concedes graciously. Smooth transfer. Elite cohesion in transition.

2024.assessment: Boakai government; some tensions with CDC opposition. Functional multi-party system.

2025.assessment: Estimated minor tensions functional; Boakai administration continuing.

1990.assessment: NPFL invasion December 1989 rapidly destabilizes country. By mid-1990 Taylor controls most of Liberia except Monrovia. Samuel Doe killed September 1990. Multiple armed factions (NPFL, AFL, INPFL). State collapse.

1991.assessment: Multiple factions control different areas: NPFL (~60%), ULIMO emerging, AFL remnants in Monrovia. ECOMOG in Monrovia. No unified state control.

1992.assessment: Operation Octopus (NPFL assault on Monrovia Oct 1992) repulsed by ECOMOG. Continued fragmented control across factions.

1993.assessment: Multiple factions: NPFL, ULIMO-K, ULIMO-J, AFL, LPC. IGNU controls Monrovia corridor. Cotonou Peace Agreement signed July 1993 but implementation fails.

1994.assessment: Linas-Marcoussis negotiations; factions continue fighting. No faction controls majority of territory. ECOMOG struggles to enforce peace.

1995.assessment: Abuja I (1995) Agreement; 6 factions. Continued fighting; state collapse persists.

1996.assessment: April 1996 Monrovia fighting between factions catastrophic. Abuja II Agreement; CFA (Cease-Fire Agreement). Disarmament begins. Still effectively state collapse.

1997.assessment: July 1997 elections: Taylor wins 75%. NPFL transitions to political party (NPP). Government begins reconstituting but armed factions remain and large areas ungoverned.

1998.assessment: Taylor government expanding control; some armed residual groups. Lofa County insecurity. Rubber and timber controlled by Taylor networks.

1999.assessment: LURD (Liberians United for Reconciliation and Democracy) invades from Guinea September 1999. Second Civil War begins. Government loses control of northern Liberia.

2000.assessment: LURD expands from Guinea; fighting in Lofa, Bong, Nimba counties. Taylor government controls Monrovia and coastal areas.

2001.assessment: LURD advances; UN sanctions on Liberia (timber, diamonds). Taylor loses control of northern and western areas.

2002.assessment: MODEL (Movement for Democracy in Liberia) opens second front from Côte d'Ivoire. Taylor controls mainly Monrovia and some coastal areas.

2003.assessment: LURD reaches Monrovia outskirts; fighting in capital June-July 2003. Taylor resigns/exiles to Nigeria August 2003. NTGL installed; CPA. UNMIL arrives October 2003. Effective state collapse during transition.

2004.assessment: UNMIL deployment (15,000 troops); DDR beginning. NTGL in Monrovia. Significant areas still not under NTGL control; factional militias persisting.

2005.assessment: UNMIL expanding control; DDR progressing. October-November 2005 elections (EJS wins). Improving territorial control but significant demobilization challenges.

2006.assessment: EJS inaugurated January 2006; government reconstituting. UNMIL supporting security sector. Some ex-combatants pose localized challenges.

2007.assessment: Improving government control; armed groups mostly demobilized. Cross-border tensions with Guinea; some mineral area disputes.

2008.assessment: Continued consolidation; security sector reform with UNMIL. Some banditry in remote areas.

2009.assessment: EJS government controls territory nationally. UNMIL downsizing but present. Minor criminal/ex-combatant challenges in western counties.

2010.assessment: Relatively stable; UNMIL continuing drawdown. Electoral preparations.

2011.assessment: Post-election violence November 2011 (CDC boycott, LNP/AFL response) creates temporary challenges. Quickly contained. EJS re-elected.

2012.assessment: Stable control. UNMIL drawdown continuing. Some cross-border spillover from Côte d'Ivoire election violence.

2013.assessment: Improving security environment; government control strengthening. UNMIL reducing presence.

2014.assessment: Ebola crisis severely strains governance capacity. Traditional authority structures disrupted. Some areas temporarily difficult to administer.

2015.assessment: Ebola declared over May 2015; government control largely maintained. UNMIL continues support.

2016.assessment: Stable territorial control; UNMIL drawdown accelerating. Government managing territory effectively.

2017.assessment: Elections Oct-Dec 2017; competitive but peaceful. George Weah wins. Stable territorial control maintained through transition.

2018.assessment: UNMIL mandate ends March 2018; Liberian security forces fully responsible. Some challenges in remote areas but overall stable.

2019.assessment: Post-UNMIL transition; Liberian security forces managing. Weah government controlling territory. Some economic grievances create protests.

2020.assessment: COVID-19 strains governance but territorial control maintained. LNP and AFL managing.

2021.assessment: Stable territorial control. Some protests over economic conditions but no security challenges.

2022.assessment: Stable; pre-election political activity. No significant territorial control challenges.

2023.assessment: October 2023 elections; Boakai wins, Weah concedes peacefully. Stable territorial control throughout.

2024.assessment: Boakai government inaugurated January 2024. Stable territorial control; peaceful transition.

2025.assessment: Estimated stable; Boakai administration. No significant territorial control challenges.

1990.assessment: Civil war; iron ore (LAMCO) operations suspended. Diamonds and rubber looted by factions. No disclosure.

1991.assessment: War; extractive revenues opaque. Factions exploiting natural resources.

1992.assessment: War; no extractive transparency.

1993.assessment: War; natural resources financing conflict.

1994.assessment: War; conflict resources.

1995.assessment: War; conflict diamonds/timber.

1996.assessment: War; natural resources controlled by factions.

1997.assessment: Taylor government; rubber/timber/diamonds revenues not disclosed. Oriental Timber Corporation (OTC) opaque concession.

1998.assessment: Taylor; opaque extractive revenues. OTC and other Taylor-linked companies exploit timber without transparency.

1999.assessment: Taylor; opaque. Supporting Sierra Leone RUF with conflict diamonds.

2000.assessment: UN Panel of Experts investigating Liberia timber and diamonds. Taylor revenues opaque.

2001.assessment: UN sanctions on Liberia diamonds (UNSCR 1343) and timber (UNSCR 1408). Revenues were opaque and sanctions-violating.

2002.assessment: Sanctions; opaque resource exploitation.

2003.assessment: Taylor exile; resource exploitation ends with war. NTGL inheriting mess.

2004.assessment: NTGL; GEMAP imposes some financial oversight on extractives. Timber and diamond revenues begin to be tracked. Minimal transparency. UN timber sanctions lifted gradually.

2005.assessment: NTGL/elections; ArcelorMittal iron ore agreement signed (September 2005). Concession terms not fully public. Minimal transparency.

2006.assessment: EJS government; timber sanctions lifted (UNSCR 1689). Forest concessions under review. No EITI yet but GEMAP oversight. Minimal transparency.

2007.assessment: Diamond sanctions lifted (UNSCR 1753). EITI discussions beginning. Some concession transparency improving with donor pressure.

2008.assessment: EJS commits to EITI; preparations begin. Liberia extractive revenues under scrutiny. Global Witness reports on Liberia concessions.

2009.assessment: Liberia becomes EITI candidate (2009). First reconciliation work beginning. Iron ore, rubber, timber, diamonds included.

2010.assessment: EITI candidate; first EITI report published. Iron ore (ArcelorMittal) revenues being reconciled. Some discrepancies found.

2011.assessment: EITI candidate; iron ore boom; multiple EITI reports. Transparency improving significantly.

2012.assessment: EITI validation ongoing; approaching compliant status. Significant iron ore revenues (ArcelorMittal, BHP).

2013.assessment: Liberia achieves EITI Compliant status (2013). One of first African states to achieve compliance. Multiple commodities disclosed: iron ore, rubber, diamonds, timber.

2014.assessment: EITI compliant; meaningful disclosures. Ebola disrupts but EITI maintained.

2015.assessment: EITI compliant; iron ore sector collapse (ArcelorMittal suspended). EITI reports reflect collapse.

2016.assessment: EITI compliant; new EITI 2016 standard implementation. SL Mining (Gerald Group) beginning.

2017.assessment: EITI compliant; multiple reports covering iron ore, rubber, diamonds.

2018.assessment: EITI compliant; Weah government continues EITI. Beneficial ownership work beginning.

2019.assessment: EITI compliant; beneficial ownership register development. EITI 2019 standard.

2020.assessment: EITI compliant; COVID-19 reduces mining. Reports continue.

2021.assessment: EITI validation 2021; Liberia receives corrective actions in areas of beneficial ownership and contract transparency. Compliant with actions required.

2022.assessment: Working on corrective actions. EITI compliance maintained with improvements needed.

2023.assessment: EITI validation; addressing corrective actions. Liberia largely meeting EITI 2023 standard. Boakai government commits to transparency.

2024.assessment: EITI compliant; Boakai anti-corruption agenda reinforces transparency.

2025.assessment: Estimated EITI compliant meaningful; ongoing improvements.
